JavaScript Core (62) — ES6 Let & Const: Let ESLint Be Your Study Buddy
If you want to take your JavaScript up a level, I'd genuinely suggest giving ESLint a try.
Learning ES6 Well — Let ESLint Help You Out
I won't rehash how to add ESLint to a project here. If you're not sure how, I'd point you at this one: "
Add ESLint to your project in five minutes

So what is ESLint? In short, it's a JavaScript style management tool. And what's a style management tool? Well, when you're actually building stuff, you'll almost always be working with a team, and everyone has different habits. For example, the original devs might be used to declaring variables with
, while people who joined later prefer ES6's
We've already gone over the differences between
plenty of times. The thing is, if a team never sets a development convention, the project can get pretty messy:

var myName = "Ray";

let maName = "is Ray not Array";
Or the classic question of whether to add a semicolon at the end:
Over time the code just gets messier and more confusing, and eventually it becomes a pain to maintain.
While you're learning, I'd also recommend the Airbnb style. Basically, the Airbnb style wants you to use ES6 syntax wherever you can, instead of ES5.
Of course, if Airbnb feels too punishing, you can start with the Standard style instead — it's a fair bit more relaxed.
And finally, I'd suggest having a read through Airbnb's official docs:

Personally I'd recommend reading the original. With a translated version, you're at the mercy of whether the author who forked it has time to keep it updated, and whether other contributors' PRs get merged back in.

Now you might be thinking — do I really have to read the whole thing before I can start writing? Nope. You can just install ESLint and learn as you go. When a warning pops up, look up the fix and figure out why the change makes sense.
One thing to note: you have to install it once per project, since ESLint analyzes things on a per-project basis.
Lastly, here's a feature I'd want you to use once you already have a decent grip on ES6: "

If you want to take your JavaScript up a level, I'd genuinely suggest giving ESLint a try. This post covers what ESLint is, why the Airbnb and Standard style guides matter, and a concrete reason why you shouldn't mix var with ES6's let and const.
